speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven. Interview with Regina Mauskopf. If I can take you back to Bergen-Belson, towards the end of your stay there, approximately how long did you-- did you stay in Bergen-Belson?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, we worked-- like February, you know, we came there. As I said, I don't remember Belson, not at all, Bergen-Belson. Nothing at all. I don't know. At that time, maybe we knew it's Belson. But for now, I don't remember, to remember Belson. And yeah, as I say, I was liberated in April on the 14th-- 15th.

speaker: interviewer
text: 15Th.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so, that's how long we'd been there, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you describe the-- the feeling you had on the day of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. The things are-- when they start, kind of-- first of all, we saw that we-- I mean, we realized that we don't see too much of the SS, that they kind of like-- not disappeared, but we don't see nobody, like they walked around, or whatever. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we heard planes going. And some-- well, some-- we used to guess, are those our planes? You know. Or we didn't know whose, but It was kind of the planes were going terrible. And then, we saw the-- that there are not too many the SS. And then, all of a sudden, there is, like, almost just heat, you know? And we-- as we were sitting, you know, there in the daytime-- you know, everybody was sitting like this, because there was no place where to kind of like to sit different. There's a lot of people. A lot-- It was kind of live with already the thing, because a lot had died out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and all of a sudden they-- we see-- hear some, you know, [MAKES OOH SOUND] outside, you know, like voices, noisy. So I-- they were sitting, the two girls there, my two nieces. And I was with her daughter, my cousin. We ran out. And then, there are people outside. And they say, the American-- the English are coming. The-- the-- we are-- we are going to be liberated. The soldiers are here. I don't know if they said Americans, because we didn't know how American looks like, or an English soldier looks like. And I-- but I don't think that the girls were out. Just I was there with Dora, the cousin. And there are soldiers and-- what's it-- some Jeeps, or tanks. I-- I-- I think it was Jeeps. I don't know. Was all kind of commotion from everybody, everybody there. And we were so happy, we grabbed whatever was on the floor, gravel, with straws, whatever. We were throwing on this, like confetti, on the soldiers. That's what-- Norman said that, when he told me-- that photographer, he was there, he [INAUDIBLE] exactly. I told him that years ago-- and when he was talking with him-- that he was there. And then it's-- and then, we run back inside, into the barracks. And I-- we are telling in the barracks, they-- whoever is still there-- the war is over. The war is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my two nieces started to cry. They huddled to each other, and they were-- and the first word-- they knew about their mother. Their mother is gone. But the first-- you know, the first word, when after that, they think, I wonder where father is? That was the first thing, but in Hungarian, they said it. I wonder where he is? I mean, in Yiddish, but father, they called in Hungarian. [NON-ENGLISH], is like daddy. So and that's what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then, when it kind of got a little bit quiet down, the whole camp broke out and to go for into the kitchen for food. And the-- in the barracks, we had-- there was this raw turnips, and potatoes, and sauerkraut in the barrels. And you-- and in the blanket, you know, everybody got a blanket and were carrying and the thing. We thought, who knows, again, what's going to happen, you know, thing. But then, they started. The next day, they started giving-- then, again, people get sick from the food, because they started getting a bit of-- fed, you know, these nourishing from the-- you know-- cans-- the conserves, we called it-- you know, with this-- all kinds of nourishing food in there. Did not agree with our stomach. And we started to get sick. They, right away, think-- start to take out for the sick one, who could not walk, and take them there-- there was a delousing with the white powder, with all kinds of stuff. And not bad, but with the white powder. And they took them. And some in hospitals and some things. And-- and then, we started to holler, the ones who were working, kind of, at the time, you wouldn't think the sick. We're going to get sick. So-- but little by little they-- And we went to Hanover. And there, they put us in-- it was military barracks. And was everybody assigned to their-- every country had their sections, the Czech, the Romanian, the Greeks, the-- from every country had their own barrack, kind of. And from there, we were-- had good food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hanover?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hanover. Yeah. Kind of military food, we got there. And we could go-- not all over. Like, we had a curfew, kind of, you know? Could go in the city, and-- well, to buy-- there was nothing to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was one thing, too. They were wondering, my two nieces-- because I was always kind of like a go-getter. Well, they were giving us kind of military food, but we didn't have something like something sour, something-- you know? It's something-- you know, it's-- it's things, like onions, or something, you know, that more-- what do you call it? like [INAUDIBLE]. Substantial? Or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Something, you know, like something sour, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pungent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Something like that. And-- and then, again, you know, some-- there were boys already too, but different, not together with the girls. And then, they were going into the city between the farmers, you know? And if the farmer didn't want to give, they went in the-- in the vegetable garden, and they took it themselves. And they started begging me. I said that-- oh, Regina, you always were good. Some-- the humanity came back. Once I was free, I could not go to bring something, a cucumber, or whatever it was that time. It really was not cucumbers, but it was the green-- you know, the onions, and the, you know, the-- I don't know. I don't remember, but something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what they told me. They said, Regina, what happened to you? I said [INAUDIBLE]. That's what had happened. Couldn't do it anymore, because we were not really hungry. We were just craving for something, you know? You know, like, for instance, here would be like grapes, or an orange, or something in the sour. Where, over there, what was something like an onion. It's kind of pinchy, kind of like, you know? Feel something. So how long did you stay in Hanover?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hanover, we stayed a few weeks in Hanover. I don't really remember. Kind of, a couple of weeks, maybe three weeks there. So we didn't know what bubble gum is, chewing gum is. And well, as I said, we had a curfew. And by the gate, there was the English people. And they were kind of chewing the gum, you know, blowing bubbles. And we was standing, looking. What? What? So he took out and explained this, what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the-- this-- was this a displaced persons camp? A DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- no, it didn't have the name. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it run by the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the military. Yeah, by the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: The British?

speaker: interviewee
text: British, I mean. Yeah. And then, they kind of-- again, you know, they were signing up, you know, which direction, like, already what check, but which direction, which, what. And then, they put us on military trucks, on the thing. And they took us. It didn't took long to get to Prague with the trucks from Hanover. I don't know why. I know it is very far from the Czech border, but [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what made you go to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the closest way from the-- from Germany, from Hanover. So we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what was your destination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Home, meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home, meaning I'll find my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- and my two nieces thought their father is going to come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Solocin?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Solocin. Yeah. And then, when we came to Prague-- and as I said, I don't remember if it was taped already. Everybody was running around. And that-- there was a guy, which he was-- you know, he told me about the-- my two brothers. And then, he went-- from there, we went to Budapest, because they say they gave an-- a few-- money for those newcomers, you know, thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Budapest. We were there a couple of days. And then, we went back on the train. We rode the train on top, because there was no place. There were soldiers. They was from all over. The war-- they was regular trains. [INAUDIBLE] The whole world was mixed up, kind of like, Russian soldiers, and-- and-- and-- all kinds, all kinds of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stopped once. We were for a whole day outside. And we had raw potatoes. And we cooked them. And then, we made fire, kind of gypsies. Good cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because-- and then, they-- from some, I don't remember which, said, am I going home from Budapest? It's pretty far. You know? And as I say, stop and go, too. They-- they gave us-- they, kind of-- some, a kitchen. I don't know from where. And they were feeding us. And then, when I came to Munkacs and find my-- was that on the tape? I don't know. A little bit of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he said-- I asked him if he saw my two brothers, or what. And so he said, what's the use, you [INAUDIBLE]. So I-- I got hysterical. A Russian soldier came over. He didn't know what happened, something that I-- we were all crying, but I was kind of like screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they told him. I could not talk. And they told him that my two brothers got killed. And he did like this. [NON-ENGLISH], you know? And walked away. To him, it was nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- but then, when I found, the second time, the same [INAUDIBLE] you know, Rozenbaum, you know, what he was together with my brothers. And he said-- then, he told me what happened to them. And so, we came, afterwards, to Munkacs. We slept over the night with them [INAUDIBLE], because it was night already. And the next day, we walked home from the same place where we were in the shul, but we were four of us. And we came home. And so, as we came home from the main road up to the village, I had to go past my brother's house, for that-- what the soldier-- you saw that thing-- his house. And that house was kind of whole. It was not, kind of-- well, it was not too much. It was not the war demolished in the village, our house, but something else. But so, over there, already, a few people which were liberated before me. So they picked my brother's house. Because, first, it was closer to the main road. And it was intact. So everybody, whoever came home, went there. Well, with me, was like this. As I walked in, in the-- there-- and I had a knapsack, a smaller one than when we left from home. And I threw that down, [VOICE BREAKING] and ran to that house. Well, not far away. It's kind of like, oh, a block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I-- and I-- I asked there-- my sister's husband came home. And I asked him, how's the-- you know-- the house there? He said, well, you'll see it. you know? That's what he told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I came to the house, in the front yard. We had that little creek was in the front yard. And-- and there are the weeds in the front yard, because we were-- it did not have grass. It was kind of like stones, the things. Up the walls, are weeds. There are no windows, no doors, no ceilings, just the roof. The stable was not there. The little-- the little, tiny thing was kind of only just half of it there. And-- [VOICE BREAKING] and I stood in the the middle of the yard and looked at the thing. And as I took a look, like in the side, all the neighbors across the street, and little children their hand, they were looking, like, somebody-- I don't know. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: But one of the neighbors, which lived kind of-- and she came too, that one which we gave a lot of-- then came another one, which she had too, my sisters, a lot of linen. I have some of the sewing stuff, you know? [INAUDIBLE] you know [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked what we were doing, once back home, the sports, or what, in the village before the war. Well, the girls had their kind of-- and there was no chores to do. We did all kinds of work, the needlepoint, and all those things. In the wintertime, we had fun. Oh, there is-- we used to have the feathers. So we used to get the-- every Saturday night, with another house. And the boys came too, if you had a boyfriend. The boys didn't do nothing. They just kept company. The girls were separating the little thing from the feathers, for the pillows, and for things. We were reading, you know, some things. Well, we didn't have movies. We had to go to [INAUDIBLE] where you had to walk. It was just Saturday night. And if it was Sabbath, you were not allowed to walk after, without the [INAUDIBLE], without an [INAUDIBLE]. But I stood there, and one came running from the other neighbor there and hugged me, and kept on saying, don't worry, don't worry. She gave me back a few pieces of stuff. And that was the homecoming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Solocin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was, already-- we came home, like, in the middle of August. So there were some people thought that we're never going to come back, because we were late. And I stayed there till-- well, Barry came, my husband came. And well, we got engaged. And then, was the holidays. He went away for business. And then, after the holidays, he came back. And in November, we-- we got married. And the marriage, there was kind of a few of the Jewish people. It was the Christian people, they were doing the dancing. We-- there was not dancing for us. And then, I was there, after the wedding. We left Solocin. I remember, like now. I stood there before the-- there was a [INAUDIBLE]-- and waiting for the wagon, because horse and wagon came to pick us up, to go back to where Barry's village. And from there, we went back with the wagon to Ungvar there. And we've been in Ungvar there. And I said goodbye to my two nieces. And they supposed to follow us in a couple of weeks with their uncle. And-- and while we were in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], you know, and we went away to [? Krakow. ?] And then we had to wait a long time, because they changed the money, you know, where we had money, we had pocket full of money. But nobody had nothing. My husband wanted to sell the watch. Nobody had money to buy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in [? Krakow, ?] because again, on account of the transportation. And one, at that time, the Russians closed up the Carpathian from the rest of the Czechoslovakia. And they remained there. So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- I never heard from them for-- until I have another [? son ?] there. It's in '47, '48, something like that. They were so happy that we have a [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a thing that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] No, that through the letter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Contact. Contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: A contact. And then, things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- after the war, we went to [NON-ENGLISH], you know, in the Sudeten. And that was in November. It was Hanukka, when we arrived there, by the time-- you know? I don't know how long it took, a week, or whatever. I really-- Hanukkah comes out always in different, according to Yiddish or the Jewish. And in November, the 24th. And so we went there. And there, the-- was in around-- it came up [NON-ENGLISH] time, you know. In March, they started talking that the Russians are going to take over the whole Czechoslovakia. So in April-- and then, my husband went to Prague to find out from other people-- I have cousins in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter. You know, in every city there. But he went to Prague there and to find out what's going on, to hear some more news about that. And they said, yeah, definitely. And on the first of May, they have, the communists, a big celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, by then, he came home. And he started packing again, fixing up again the knapsacks and suitcases, and left everything there on the floor again, because we had already some, we found something there. You know? We got kind of established in those-- during the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Barry had his-- my husband had his two sisters with him, and a brother, David, [INAUDIBLE], if you want to know him. And one of the sisters got married there, in [NON-ENGLISH] there, where my [? brother-in-law ?] were living. And so there we were again on the road. And that, too, kind of-- you-- they said, you know, that even the Czechs, you know, the good ones, you know, when it came to the border, they're taking away, if you have gold. I had this ring. And I had a man's ring, you know, with the monogram. It was real heavy ones. I had a earrings. And well, the ring, the wedding band, that-- that, they let through. But all the other things--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said that people put in-- opened the toothpaste in the bottom and squished them slowly, not directly, you know, into the toothpaste. But they found out, so the thing. I put one, this one, on a toe, you know, in the shoes, you know, in the toe. The other ones-- yeah, they were cut-- and they put-- they used to bake in the bread, but then they cut off the bread, you know? So when we came there to the border, the Czech border, to go back to Germany, because we wanted to immigrate, wherever, to Israel, to wherever, and [CLEARS THROAT] so went in, they found the-- the-- those two big rings. I had the silverware, so they took off a set for themselves. The one guy, he took just kind of like one set, kind of like a knife, and a spoon, and fork, and the things. So from where--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: From where did you-- did you leave for the United States? From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] took us through the Czech border, through a thing. And we went by train. And then, we were hidden in a-- in a lumber thing there, because we were not legal to Germany, again. Because that time, was already established there some kind of legality. I don't know what.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- we went in Munich. There was the-- the DP camp, the big one. Everybody got there. Eisenhower-- I remember, Eisenhower came there, looked us over. And we'd been there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, they assigned us to-- to different camps. So we went to Wasserburg. And we went there. We were in Germany for two and a half years. And there was another story there where we went to-- and my husband, on account of the marriage license, because we could not get the-- the visa. We didn't have the-- we could not show-- it was not allowed to go back, you know, to home, because that-- we just had [NON-ENGLISH], the marriage license, in-- in the Yiddish. And so, we had-- they gave us an address. We had to make up a story, that we were married before the war. And the-- the thing got burned somewheres, from-- we could not get the-- the marriage license. And the papers, in the war, got lost. And he came looking for me in Germany, found me in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how we went. And so, we got some papers. But then, that was not enough. When we came, we didn't got the papers. Everybody get the papers, and they're going-- we almost went on boat to Israel-- what they turned back on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But first, we saw that the-- he had David and the sister. We saw first, that they should be away-- especially his sister. Louise, got married there in [NON-ENGLISH]. They got a baby, a small one. So we let them go wherever they-- and we were the last ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, we saw we are not getting nothing from Munich, the papers, the visa, nothing. And there was another couple didn't. So we went to Munich. We went to the consulate, to the American. And-- and he said to-- to us, do you know what? In America is a law that-- we told him that we are married, but we haven't got the marriage license. That's what our papers state there. He said, there, you could marry-- get married twice with the same wife, even if they are divorced. And you could get married there. So we-- and they give us there the secretary. She said there-- and there was another couple-- she said, you come tomorrow morning, or-- or whenever, you know? And you're going to get a divorce. We're going to wait for that couple, the-- the witness. And they're going to be for us. It cost us a few cartons of cigarettes, you know, because that's-- there was no money to pay. We didn't have. And so, that's what we did. We were laughing because, in the morning, we got a divorce. And then we went for lunch. And then the men started laughing. They said, well, we are free now. We won't re-marry you. We're not-- we won't marry you. You know? Well, they was kind of-- Eventually-- eventually, though, you came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got married. Yeah. And then, we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Thanksgiving, something. Oh, we had-- oh, we had--

speaker: interviewee
text: '47. We were in a hurricane. Our ship was almost cut in two. Everything was broken. I had another problem there. I was pregnant, and I had a miscarriage right at-- I-- on-- on-- on the-- the third day, when we went on the boat. And there was no doctor. It was a military boat, the was the name of that thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The men were sleeping separate. We slept in hammocks, because it was military, you know? Through the day, we were together. But for sleeping time, the men had their quarters, and we had ours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started losing the baby. And there was-- there was nothing to do. There was a little room, kind of like, you know, just for somebody wasn't feeling good. They packed ice on me, and everything. And that was the-- on account of that big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hurricane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hurricane. Instead, I don't know, we're supposed to be like, I don't know, two weeks there, or whatever. We were more than three weeks on-- on that thing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you finally settle when you arrived to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Brooklyn. They had a-- a brother there. Well, it was a step-brother, just by my-- by marriage. So we were there for a week. And one-- my husband started to work right away, because it's-- he came Thursday.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took me right in the hospital. I had-- I-- I almost died from blood poisoning, because they-- all that kind of accumulated inside, because I-- I lost the baby the night before we came to New York, you know? And it had to be the rest of it done. So I went in the hospital, till Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, he went-- my husband went to work. There was kind of like a-- we knew the people from the old country in the factory thing. So and that brother of my husband wrote a little slip and told for the thing, if he-- told him how to go this way, that way. And if he gets lost, he should just show it to the police, because he could not talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went there, into the factory. And he was-- everybody was wondering how he came, without language, there, you know, already, on the train, New York, on the subways, with all the things. And so-- That's the end of tape number seven.
